text: CHARLOTTE'S BAY, or MARGARET'S BAY, an indentation of the SE coast of Nova Scotia, Hants co., 15 m. SW of Halifax, in N lat. 44° 35′, W long. 63° 57′.

CHARLOTTE'S ISLAND (QUEEN), an island of the South Pacific, in the Low archipelago, in S lat. 19° 20′, W long. 138° 45′. It is 6 m. in length, and about 1 in breadth; and is flat and sandy, but produces cotton, dates, &c. Its coasts are destitute of shelter. It was discovered by Wallis in 1767.

CHARLOTTE'S ISLANDS (QUEEN), a group in the South Pacific, to the N of or sometimes included in the archipelago of the New Hebrides, extending between 8° 30' and 12° 15' S lat., and 165° and 170° E long. The principal islands of the group are Santa Cruz, Egmont or Nilendi, Vaniforo or Pitt's island, Tucopia or Barwell island, Mitre, Cherry, Swallow, Duff's islands, and Toubouai or Ourry. They are of volcanic origin, are generally fertile, and are inhabited by Negroes. They were discovered in 1595 by Mendana, and named by him Santa Cruz. In 1767 they were visited by the English navigator Carteret, who, ignorant of their previous discovery, gave the name of Queen Charlotte to the group, and that of Egmont to the largest island.

CHARLOTTE'S POINT, or CHAO-PHING-TCHU, a headland of China, in Leaou-tung or Shing-king, forming the S extremity of the peninsula called Regent's Sword, and the N side of the entrance of the gulf of Chih-le.
CHARLOTTE'S POINT (QUEEN), the SE extremity of the island of New Caledonia, in the group of the New Hebrides, South Pacific, in S lat. 22° 15'.

CHARLOTTE'S SOUND (QUEEN), a channel of British North America, which separates the N part of Quaqua and Vancouver's island from the continent, and terminates on the SE in the gulf of Georgia. It contains numerous islands, forming Brough- | the p. of Bellingham, Northumberland. Pop. 176. CHA ton's archipelago, and the groups of Galiano and Waldes.

CHARLOTTE'S SOUND (QUEEN), a bay of New Zealand, on the NE coast of the Middle island, and on the W side of Cook strait, in S lat. 41° 5′ 57". It is perfectly land-locked, being surrounded by hills enclosing deep and finely-wooded valleys, and small bays or coves which rarely contain more than half a sq. m. in area of flat land.

CHARLOTTESVILLE, a village, cap. of Albemarle co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., on Moore's creek, 2 m. above its entrance into Rivanna river, 85 m. NW of Richmond. It is irregularly but well built, chiefly of brick. It derives its chief importance from being the seat of the university of Virginia. Pop. in 1840, about 1,000.—Also a village of Hancock co., in the state of Indiana, on the E side of a branch of Blue river, 28 m. E of Indianapolis.

CHARLOTTE TOWN, a parish and town on the SW side of Prince Edward island, in Queen's co. The town, which is the seat of government and metropolis of the island, is built on a gently rising ground on the N bank of the Hillsborough river; and presents a pleasing appearance from the river and harbour. It is well laid out, with spacious squares and wide streets at right angles; but these are as yet thinly occupied by houses. The houses, with the exception of five or six, are all of wood. No part of the town exceeds 50 ft. above high water; but the land gradually rises behind it to the height of 150 ft. at the distance of 1\frac{1}{2} m.; and is well cultivated. Pop. about 5,000. The harbour, which is at the bottom of Hillsborough bay, is about a 1 m. in breadth at the entrance, and widens into a capacious haven. The tide extends its influence 20 m. above the town; and the river is navigable for the largest ships to the distance of 7 or 8 m.

CHARLTON, a hamlet in the p. and 1 m. NE of Wantage, Berks, on the Berks and Wilts canal. Pop. 252.—Also a tything in the p. of Henbury, Gloucestershire, 6 m. N of Bristol. Pop. 329.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Andover, Hants. Pop. 323 .- Also a hamlet partly in the parishes of King's Sutton and Newbottle, Northamptonshire. Pop. 446. -Also a hamlet in the p. of Kilmersdon, Somerset. Pop. 241.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Singleton, Sussex. Pop. 223.—Also a tything in the p. of Downton, Wilts. Pop. 300.—Also a parish of Wilts, 2 m. NE of Malmsbury, now included within its new boundary. Area 5,940 acres. Pop. 683.—Also a parish of Wilts, 4 m. SW of Pewsey, near the W bank of the Avon. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 201. -Also a hamlet in the p. of Crossthorn, Worcestershire. Pop. 312.

CHARLTON, a township of Worcester co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 53 m. WSW of Boston. Its surface is hilly, but its soil is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 2,117.—Also a township of Saratoga co., in the state of New York, 25 m. NNW of Albany. It possesses a hilly surface, and is drained by a branch of the Mohawk river. The soil, consisting of clay and sandy loam, is generally fertile. Pop. 1,933. The village contained in 1840 about 300 inhabitants.
CHARLTON-ABBOTS, a parish of Gloucestershire, 3 m. SE of Winchcombe. Area 2,190 acres. Pop. 101.
CHARLTON-ADAM, or East Charlton, a parish of Somerset, 7 m. NE of Castlecary. Area with Charlton-Mackrell, 3,910 acres. Pop. 472.
CHARLTON-NEAR-DOVER, a parish of Kent, N of and mostly in the Cinque port liberty of Dover. Area 190 acres. Pop. 1,472.
CHARLTON-EAST-QUARTER, a township in
CHARLTON-HORETHORNE, a parish of Somerset, 5\frac{1}{4} m. SW of Wincanton. Area 2,340 acres. Pop. 569.
CHARLTON-KINGS, a parish of Gloucestershire, 2 m. SSE of Cheltenham. Area 5,020 acres. Pop. 3,232.
CHARLTON-MACKRELL, a parish of Somerset, about a m. E of Somerton, on the Carey. Area with Charlton-Adam. Pop. 405.
CHARLTON-MARSHALL, a parochial chapelry of Dorset, 2 m. S of Blandford-Forum; bounded on the E by the Stour. Area 2,100 acres. Pop. 395.
CHARLTON-MUSGRAVE, a parish of Somerset, 1 m. NNE of Wincanton, on the Cale. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. 409.
CHARLTON (NORTH), a township in the p. of Ellingham, Northumberland, 61 m. NW of Alnwick. Pop. 238.
CHARLTON-UPON-OTMOOR, a parish of Oxfordshire, 5 m. S of Bicester, E of the Roman road, and bounded on the S and E by the Ray. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 658.
CHARLTON (South), a township in the p. of Ellingham, Northumberland, 5\frac{1}{2} m. NW of Alnwick. Pop. 188.
CHARLTON-WEST-QUARTER, a township of Bellingham parish, Northumberland. Pop. 176.
CHARLTON-WOODLANDS, a hamlet in the p. of Shepton-Mallet, Somerset. Pop. 86.
CHARLTON-NEXT-WOOLWICH, a parish of Kent, 8 m. SE of London, bounded on the N by the Thames. Area 1,250 acres. Pop. 2,655.
CHARLWOOD, a parish of Surrey, 61 m. SSW of Reigate, and intersected by the London and Horsham railway. Area 6,290 acres. Pop. 1,291.

CHARLY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Chateau- Thierry. The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,220; in 1841, 12,356. The town is situated near the r. bank of the Marne; and rises in an amphitheatral form on a hill which is crowned by an old castle, 8 m. SW of Chateau-Thierry. Pop. 5,200. It contains manufactories of cloth, serge, and buttons, and several distilleries; and has a considerable commerce in grain, wine, horses, sheep, cattle, pigs, hosiery, mercery, &c.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Cher, cant. of Nerondes. Pop. 785. It contains quarries of freestone.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Saint-Genis-Laval. Pop. 1,015.
CHARMANT, a village of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. and 5 m. W of Lavallette and 11 m. S of Angoulême. Pop. 610. It has 6 annual fairs. In the environs is a copper-mine.
CHARME', a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Aigre. Pop. 1,175.
CHARM-EL-KOMAN, a bay and port of Upper Egypt, on the shore of the Red sea, 110 m. SE of Kosseir, in N lat. 24° 44'.

CHARMES, or CHARMES - SUR - MOSELLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, arrond. of Mirecourt. The cant. comprises 27 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,008; in 1841, 12,864. The town is prettily situated on the l. bank of the Moselle, which is here crossed by a handsome bridge, 12 m. NE of Mirecourt, and on the road from Epinal to Nancy. Pop. in 1841, 2,883. It contains extensive tanneries, and manufactories of lace; and has a considerable trade in timber, grain, wine, leather, lace, and gypsum.
CHARMES - EN - L'ANGLE, or CHARMES - LAPetite, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Doulevant, 12 m. from Vassy. Pop. 179. It contains some blast-furnaces and forges. CHA

CHARMES - LA - GRANDE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Doulevant, 11 m. SSE of Vassy. Pop. 606. It contains some blast-furnaces and forges. CHARMEY, CHARMAY, or GALMIS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 15 m. S of Freyburg, and 5 m. NE of Gruyere, in a valley of the same name, at an alt. of 3,000 ft. above sea-level. Pop. (Cath.) 710. The locality is celebrated for its cheese. In the vicinity is the ancient chartreuse of Val-Sainte or Heligenthal, suppressed in 1778.
CHARMINSTER, a parish of Dorset, 2½ m. NNW of Dorchester, on a branch of the Froome. Area 4,910 acres. Pop. 827.
CHARMOILLE, or CHALMITZ, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, bailiwick and 7 m. E of Porentrui, on the Halle. Pop. 1,321.
CHARMOIS-L'ORGUILLEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Xertigny, 12 m. SW of Epinal. Pop. 1,168.
CHARMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Marne, cant. of Heiltz-le-Maurupt, 19 m. NE of Vitry-le-Francais. Pop. 1,162.
CHARMOUTH, a parish of Dorset, 2 m. NE of Lyme-Regis. Area 790 acres. Pop. 620. The ancient village of C. is at the entrance of the Char into the Bristol channel. See LYME-REGIS.
CHARNAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Macon. Pop. 1,663. It produces good wine.
CHARNDON, a hamlet in the p. of Twyford, Bucks, 7 m. WSW of Winslow. Area 1,880 acres. Pop. 190.
CHARNECA, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, comarca and 3 m. N of Lisbon. Pop. 704. It has an annual fair.
CHARNE'CLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Rives, 21 m. from Saint- Marcellin. Pop. 1,344.
CHARNEMAGHI', or SURNAMURKI, a river of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, which takes its rise in a branch of the Eastern Ghauts; runs NE; and falls into the bay of Bengal, 70 m. N of Madras.
CHARNES, a township in the p. of Eccleshall, Staffordshire, near the Sow. Area 570 acres. Pop. 98.
CHARNEUX, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 12 m. NW of Verviers. Pop. 2,264. It contains an ancient abbey, and has manufactories of cloth, and woollen and fulling-mills.
CHARNEY, a chapelry in the p. of Longworth, Berks, 42 m. NNW of Wantage, N of the Ock. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 275.
CHARNHAM-STREET, a tything in the p. of Hungerford, Wilts. Area 2,490 acres. Pop. 401.
CHARNIZAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Preully, 18 m. S of Loches, on the l. bank of the Egronne. Pop. 1,602.
CHARNOCK-HEATH, a township in the p. of Standish, Lancashire, 3 m. SE of Chorley. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 1,062.
CHARNOCK-RICHARD, a township in the p. of Standish, Lancashire, 3 m. WSW of Chorley. Area 2,070 acres. Pop. 784.
CHARNY-LE-BACHOT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aube, cant. of Mery-sur-Seine, 12 m. from Arcis-sur-Aube. Pop. 305. It possesses manufactories of hosiery.

CHARNY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, arrond. of Verdun-sur-Meuse. The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,721; in 1841, 9,974. The town is on the l. bank of the Meuse, 4 m. N of Verdun-sur-Meuse. Pop. 441. It has two annual fairs.
CHARO, a small town of Mexico, in the prov. of Michoacan, 17 m. ESE of Valladolid, at an alt. of | it contained 500 Turkish, 50 Greek, and 50 Arme- 6,230 ft. above sea-level.

CHAROLAIS, formerly a district and county of France, in the prov. of Burgundy, now comprised in the dep. of the Saone-et- Loire. It derived its name from its cap. Charolles. It passed from the dukes of Burgundy to Spain, and was definitively reunited to France at the peace of the Pyrenees.

CHAROLLES, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Saoneet-Loire. The arrond, comprises an area of 248,737 hectares, and contains 13 cant., viz.: Bourbon-Lancy, Saint-Bonnet-de-Joux, C., Chauffailles, Clayette, Digoin, Gueugnon, Guiche, Marcigny, Palinges, Paray-le-Monial, Semur-en-Brionnais, Toulon-sur-Arroux. Pop. in 1831, 120,761; in 1841, 125,077; in 1846, 128,332.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,182; in 1841, 12,684.—The town, formerly the cap. of the ancient county of Carolais, is pleasantly situated at the confluence of the Semence and Reconce, 32 m. WNW of Macon, and 40 m. S of Autun. Pop. in 1789, 2,626; in 1821, 2,988; in 1831, 2,984; in 1836, 3,226; in 1841, 3,357. It contains a communal college and a public library, and has several manufactories of pottery, some blast-furnaces, and a printing-press. It possesses a considerable trade in wine and timber, the produce of the locality, corn, cattle, iron, and coal. A fair is held monthly. In the vicinity are the ruins of the ancient castle of the counts of Charolais.

CHARONNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of Pantin, adjacent to the E barrier of Paris. Pop. in 1846, 6,017. It contains manufactories of wax and paper. The environs contain quarries of limestone.

CHAROST, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, arrond. of Bourges. - The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,222. The com., 16 m. SW of Bourges, on the Arnon, had a pop. of 1,239 in 1841.
CHARPENTRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Varennes, 18 m. WNW of Verdun. Pop. 195.
CHARPEY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Drôme, cant. of Bourge-du-Peage. Pop. 2,770. Silk is cultivated in the environs.
CHARQUEMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Marche, 20 m. S of Montbeliard. Pop. 1,029.
CHARRACK. See KARAK.
CHARRATA, a town of Afghanistan, 9 m. W of Dera-Ghazi-Khan, in about N lat. 30° 3'. Pop. 1,000.
CHARRON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Marans. Pop. 958.—Also a commune and v. in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Evaux. Pop. 1,346.

CHARROUX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Chantelle. Pop. 1,739. -Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Vienne. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,779. The com., 6 m. SE of Civiay, had a pop. of 1,709 in 1841.

CHARRUAS, a savage tribe of Indians who inhabit the banks of the Uraguay, on the N frontier of the Banda Oriental, in South America. They are well-made, with regular features, and black eyes; but have neither been civilized nor subdued to the present time. They are excellent horsemen, and are armed with a spear from 10 to 12 ft. in length, a lasso, and a bow, or a sling.
CHARS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Marines, on the Viosne, 12 m. NW of Pontoise. Pop. 1,155.
CHARSFIELD, a parish in Suffolk, 31 m. WNW of Market-Wickham. Area 1,650 acres. Pop. 551.

CHARSHUMBA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the district of Janikli, on the Jekel-Irmak river, 10 m. above its mouth, and 28 m. NE of Samsun. It has little resemblance to a town, each particular house being surrounded by a large garden of fruittrees. When Kinneir visited the town in May 1814, II. #チタン ハトリタ ルデディ ジェン トゥルドウン・ディトリ アルカン・アク カカ カカ・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア・ア CHA nian families. He found the river at that date 250 paces wide where crossed by a bridge between the Greek and the Turk quarters of the town.
CHART (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes in Kent. The former, 2 m. W by S of Ashford, has an area of 3,190 acres. Pop. 714. The latter, 2 m. SW of Charing, has an area of 1,590 acres. Pop. 300.
CHART-NEXT-SUTTON-VALENCE, a parish in Kent, 42 m. SSE of Maidstone. Area 2,100 acres. Pop. 604.
CHARTER-HOUSE-HINTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. SSE of Bath. Area 2,890 acres. Pop. 797.
CHARTER-HOUSE-ON-MENDIP, a village in the p. of Witham, Somerset, 5 m. E of Axbridge. Pop. 99.
CHARTHAM, a parish in Kent, 3 m. SW of Canterbury. Area 4,990 acres. Pop. 974. The v. of C. is situated on the Stour river. Its church is an ancient and spacious edifice.
CHARTIER (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Indre, cant. and 5 m. N of La Chatre. Pop. 1,024.

CHARTIERS, a township in Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 6 m. N of Washington, on a creek of the same name, which falls into the Ohio below Pittsburg, after a course of 30 m. Pop. 1,616.

CHARTRE-SUR-LOIR (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, arrond. of Saint-Calais.—The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 10,741.—The com. and town, on the l. bank of the Loir, 16 m. SSW of Saint-Calais, had a pop. of 1,628 in 1841.

CHARTRES, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Eure-et- Loir.—The arrond. has an area of 193,005 hectares. Pop. in 1831, 104,136; in 1846, 109,812. It comprises the 7 cantons of Auneau, C., Courville, Illiers, Janville, Maintenon, and Voves .- The cant. comprises 35 com., and had a pop. of 34,769 in 1831.—The com. and town are seated on the river Eure, on the road from Paris to Nantes, 54 m. SW of Paris, in N lat. 48° 26′ 53″, E long. 1° 30′, at an alt. of 150 metres or 492 ft. above sea-level. It is divided into upper and lower towns, which are united by very steep streets. The principal public edifices are in the upper town, and the best squares and streets. The finest edifice is the cathedral, which surmounts the hill on which the city stands, and has two spires, one of 342, the other of 378 French ft., which render it visible from a great distance. It is "a noble and surpassing edifice," 396 French ft. in length, in the interior; 195 ft. in breadth at the transept; and 106 ft. in height to the point of the vaulted roof. The stained windows are very beautiful; and the whole adornments rich and imposing in the extreme. Some of the other churches are likewise handsome edifices. C. is the seat of a bishop, whose diocese comprises the dep. of Eure-et-Loir; and has a tribunal of commerce, a communal college, a normal school, a botanic garden, and a public library of 30,000 vols. It is a place of active commerce, and from its markets large supplies are drawn for Paris. Its grain and wool markets are among the largest in France. Its manufactures consist chiefly of hosiery, leather, and earthenware. The pates-de-gibier of C. are celebrated. Pop. in 1789, 9,664; in 1846, 15,582. -C. is a very ancient place. It is supposed to occupy the site of the Autricum of Cæsar. It was destroyed by the Normans in 858. During the reign of Charles VI. and of Charles VII., it was in the hands of the English, from whom it was wrested in 1432 by Dunois. In 1591 it was taken by Henry IV. Louis bestowed the county of C. upon his brother, the duke of Orleans.—The poet Regnier, the mathematician Pierre Nicole, the revolutionists Brissot and Petion, and General Marceau, were natives of C.
CHARTRETTES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Le Chatelet, near the r. bank of the Seine. Pop. 504. Excellent wines are made here.

CHARTREUSE (LA GRANDE), a celebrated monastery of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of St. Laurent-du-Pont, 14 m. N of Grenoble. This edifice, situated amid rugged mountains, at an alt. of 4,268 ft. above sea-level, was founded in 1084 or 1086 by Saint Bruno; and became the seat of the Chartreux or Carthusian monks. It has been repeatedly burned. The present buildings enclose an oblong square, 714 ft. in length, around which the cells of the fathers, 80 in number, are ranged. The village of Saint-Pierre-Chartreuse lies about 1 m. to the SE. The surrounding mountains contain varieties of fine marble.
CHARTREUVE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Braine, 17 m. SE of Soissons. Pop. 476.
CHARTRIDGE, or CHARTERIDGE, a hamlet in the p. of Chesham, Bucks. Pop. 404.
CHARWAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Khandeish, on an affluent of the Sutka, 30 m. S of Hindia.
CHARWELTON, a parish of Northamptonshire, 5 m. SSW of Daventry. Area 2,770 acres. Pop. 227.

CHARYBDIS, a celebrated whirlpool, just outside the port, or at the back of the tongue of land facing the port of Messina, in the straits of Messina. It is not exactly opposite to Scylla; but lies in a slanting line further S, and its centre is about 8 or 9 m. from the rock. At certain times, the sea forms violent whirlpools here. Smyth describes it as an agitated portion of the sea, from 70 to 90 fath. in depth, and circling in quick eddies which seem to be caused by the meeting of the harbour and lateral currents with the main current in the strait; but the water is often perfectly calm, or presents only a slight circular motion on the surface; and the terrors ascribed by the ancients to the navigation of this strait appear to have been in a great degree the creatures of poetic fancy.
CHARZOW, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, regency of Oppeln, circle and 5 m. SSE of Beuthen. It contains an extensive zinc work.
CHASCOMUS, a town of La Plata, in the prov. and 70 m. SSE of Buenos Ayres, on a lake of the same name.
CHASEWATER, a hamlet, 5 m. SW of Truro, in Cornwall. There are rich copper-mines in this vicinity.
CHASGO, or Shushgao, a cluster of villages, with a mud fort, 13\frac{3}{4} m. N of Ghuzni, in Afghanistan, on the road to Cabul, at an alt. of 1,274 ft. above Ghuzni, and 9,000 ft. above sea-level.

CHASI-LUNBU, or TESHU-LUMBU, a canton and town of Tibet, in the prov. of Wei, or Lassa. The town is situated in a plain, surrounded by high rustcoloured hills and mountain-ridges, and intersected by the Panomchicu, in N lat. 29° 7'. It consists of from 300 to 400 houses, inhabited by geelongs or monks; besides numerous temples, mausoleums, nunneries, and the palace of the Bantchinerdeni Lama.

CHASMA, or CSASMA, a town of Hungary, in Military Croatia, in the circle and 16 m. S of Kreutz, and 14 m. SW of Belovar, on the l. bank of a river of the same name. Pop. 1,000. The river C. takes its rise in the mountains on the confines of Sclavonia, and after making a circuit of 66 m., falls into the Lonya, 14 m. SSW of Chasma. Its principal affluent is the Glogovnicza. CHA
CHASPINHAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, cant. of Le Puy. Pop. 1,085.
CHASQUEIRO, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, which falls into Lake Mirim, to the S of the confluence of the Piratin.

CHASSAGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Nolay, 12 m. from Beaume. Pop. 964. It produces excellent wine.—Also a hamlet in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. and 11 m. SW of Anse, and 6 m. S of Villefranche. Pop. 212. It produces good wine.-Also a village in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. and 8 m. NNW of Ardes, and 10 m. SW of Issoire. In the environs is a mine of antimony.—Also a village in the dep. of Doubs, 20 m. SSE of Besançon. Pop. 300.
CHASSAL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. and 6 m. SW of Saint-Claude. Pop. 290. It contains extensive marble-quarries.
CHASSELAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of La Chapelle-de-Guinchay, 9 m. SW of Macon. Pop. 360. It is celebrated for its wines.
CHASSELAY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Limonest, 9 m. NNW of Lyon. Pop. 1,100. Lead is wrought in the locality.
CHASSENAY, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Nièvre, cant. and 4 m. S of Decize, and com. of Saint-Germain-de-Chassenay. Pop. 50. It contains a blast-furnace and forges.

CHASSENEUIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. of Saint-Georges-les-Baillargeaux. Pop. 1,054.—Also a commune and town in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Saint-Claude, 19 m. SW of Confolens, on the l. bank of the Dronne. Pop. 1,821. It has a monthly fair.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Indre, cant. of Argenton-sur- Creuse. Pop. 1,053.

CHASSENON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Chabanais, 15 m. SSE of Confolens. Pop. 1,025. CHASSERAL [Germ. Gestler or Ghestlerberg], a mountain of Switzerland, in the cant. and 22 m. NW of Berne, between the valley of Saint-Imier and Lake Bienne. It forms one of the loftiest summits of the main ridge of the Jura, rising to the height of 5,305 ft. above sea-level, and is covered with snow during ten months of the year. It is celebrated for the extent and magnificence of the prospect which it commands, and for the luxuriance of its pasturage. It abounds with alpine plants of great beauty.

CHASSERON, or Sucheron, a lofty summit of the Jura chain, extending from the dep. of the Doubs, in France, into the Swiss cant. of Vaud, between the valleys of the Sainte-Croix and Buttes. It rises to the height of 1,610 metres, or 5,280 ft.
CHASSEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. of Gondrecourt, 19 m. from Commercy. Pop. 360. It contains blast-furnaces and forges.
CHASSEY-LE'S-MONTBOZON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Saone, cant. of Montbozon. Pop. 895. It contains blast-furnaces and forges.
CHASSIERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. and 3 m. N of Largentiere. Pop. 1,369.
CHASSIGNOLLES, a village of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, cant. and 8 m. E of Auzon. In the environs are mines of lead and antimony.
CHASSIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Prauthoy. Pop. 608.

CHASSIRON, or La Tour-de-Chassiron, a hamlet and lighthouse of France, in the Charente-Inferieure, com. of Saint-Denis, at the N extremity of the Isle-d'Oléron, on the Pertuis d'Antioche, 17 m. SW of La Rochelle, in N lat. 46° 2′ 52", E long. 1° 24' 29". It is distinguished by its fixed light from the light of Cordonan.
CHASSORS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Jarnac, 17 m. WNW of Angouleme. Pop. 1,019. It contains distilleries, and has a considerable trade in brandy.
CHASTEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Saignes. Pop. 1,269.
CHASTLETON, or CHASTLEDON, a parish of Oxfordshire, 5 m. NW of Chipping-Norton, near the post-road. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 239.
CHASTREIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Latour. Pop. 1,019.

CHAT, a river of Russia, in the gov. of Toula, which rises near the sources of the Don, in two headstreams, and flows in a NW course to the Oripa, which it joins on the l. bank, 9 m. ESE of Toula. By the Ivanoskoi canal, it is united with the Don.

CHAT (Lac-Du-), an expansion of the Ottawa river, between Upper and Lower Canada. It is 16 m. in length, and in some places 2 m. broad. The chief of the Macnabs is located with his clan on the banks of this lake.

CHAT (MONT-DU-), a mountain of Savoy, forming a steep sharp ridge, which extends from several miles SW of Chamberry to the W bank of the lake of Bourget, into which it dips at its S extremity, at an angle of 60°, affording no space for a road or even a landing-place. Its alt. above sea-level is about 5,000 ft. Its summit commands a fine view of the rich vale of Chamberry, and extending on the other side to Lyons. The principal pass across it leads from the v. of Chevalu in France, to Bourget in the plain of Chamberry, by the route which Hannibal pursued from the Rhone into Italy. The v. of Chevalu has been identified with the Leviscum of Polybius; and Chamberry occupies the site of the ancient Lemincum.
CHATAHOOCHEE. See CHATTAHOOCHEE.

CHATAIGNERAIE (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vendée, arrond. of Fontenay-le-Comte. The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 19,236; in 1841, 20,001. The town is pleasantly situated in a fertile locality, 15 m. N of Fontenay-le-Comte. Pop. 1,620. It possesses considerable manufactories of woollen fabrics and paper, and has some trade in corn, cattle, and wool.
CHATAI'N-SUR-CHARENTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. of Charroux, 13 m. from Civray, on the Charente. Pop. 1,052. It produces excellent chestnuts.
CHATAUQUE. See CHAUTAUQUE.
CHATAVA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Podolia, district and 7 m. N of Kamienetz.
CHATBURN, a township in the p. of Whalley, in Lancashire, 3 m. ENE of Clitheroe. Area 720 acres. Pop. 500.
CHA-TCHOU, a town and fortress of Mongolia, on the Sirgaldzin river, which flows into the Karanor, in N lat. 40° 25', E long. 95° 20'. It is chiefly inhabited by Chinese.

CHATEAU (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, arrond. of Marennes. The cant. comprises 3 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,544; in 1841, 6,270. The town, called also Le Chateau d'Oleron, is situated at the SE extremity of the island of Oleron, on the Passede - Maumusson, or the Courreaux - d'Oleron, - the channel by which the island is separated from the continent,-6 m. NW of Marennes, and 27 m. S of La Rochelle. Pop. 3,135. It is defended by a fortress; and has a small port, the trade of which consists in salt, wine, brandy, and grain.
CHATEAU-ARNOUX, a commune of France, CHA in the dep. of the Basses-Alpes, cant. of Volonne, on the r. bank of the Durance, 9 m. SSE of Sisteron.
CHATEAU-BILAIS, or RICHMOND BAY, a town of the island of St. Vincent, in the parish of St. David, on a bay and at the mouth of a river of the same name, 9 m. NNW of Kingstown.

CHATEAUBOURG, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of Vitré. The eant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,235; in 1841, 9,265.—The town is on the r. bank of the Vilaine, 12 m. W of Vitré. Pop. 1,296. It has three annual fairs. In the environs are quarries of slate.

CHATEAUBRIANT, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure. The arrond. comprises an area of 139,172 hect.; and contains 7 cant., viz.: Châteaubriant, Derval, Saint Julien-de-Vouvantes, Moisdonla-Riviere, Nort, Nozay, and Rougé. Pop. in 1831, 62,242; in 1841, 64,261.—The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,785; in 1841, 8,728. — The town stands on the l. bank of the Cher, 29 m. NNW of Ancenis, and 40 m. N of Nantes. Pop. in 1789, 2,600; in 1821, 3,316; in 1831, 3,709; in 1836, 3,634; in 1841, 3,732. It is celebrated for its comfitures; and has manufactories of common woollen fabrics, several tanneries, and a printing establishment. Its trade consists chiefly in grain, iron, and coal. Fairs are held four times a-year. In the vicinity are the ruins of the castle in which Françoise de Foix, countess of Chateaubriand, died in 1537.

CHATEAU-CHALON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. of Voiteur, on the r. bank of the Seille, 7 m. NNE of Lons-le-Saunier. Pop. 677. It was formerly celebrated for its abbey. The environs produce good wine.
CHATEAU-CHERVIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Saint- Germain-les-Belles, 12 m. NE of Saint-Yrieix. Pop. 1,658.

CHATEAU-CHINON, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Nièvre. The arrond. comprises an area of 167,779 hect.; and contains 5 cant., viz.: Château- Chinon, Chatillon-en-Bazois, Luzy, Montsauche, and Moulins-en-Gilbert. Pop. in 1831, 58,443; in 1841, 66,098; in 1846, 68,087.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,195; in 1841, 16,896.—The town-called also Chateau-Chinon-Ville, and during the republic Chinon-la-Montagne-rises in an amphitheatral form, near the l. bank of the Yonne, 40 m. E of Nevers, and 27 m. WNW of Autun. Pop. in 1846, 2,900. It is well built, and contains the ruins of an ancient fortress. It has some manufactories of common cloth and linen, and a printing establishment. Its trade, which is considerable, consists in grain, wine, horses, cattle, pigs, leather, wool, and timber. Fairs are held seven times a-year. It was taken and sacked by the royalists in 1591.
CHATEAU - CHINON - CAMPAGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nièvre, cant. of Château-Chinon. Pop. 1,399.
CHATEAU-CORNET. See SAINT-PIERRE.

CHATEAU-DE-JOUX, a fortress of France, in the dep. and near the r. bank of the Doubs, cant. and 2 m. SSE of Pontarlier, on a steep mountain, on the Swiss frontier. It sustained, in the 17th century, a siege of 15 days.

CHATEAU-DES-PRE'S, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. of Saint-Laurent, 12 m. from Saint-Claude. Pop. 293. It has extensive manufactories of cabinet-work. CHATEAUX-D'ŒX, CHATEAU-D'OYES, OF OSCH, a parish and town of Switzerland, cap. of the district of the Pays-d'Enhaut-Romand, in the cant. de Vaud on the r. bank of the Sarine, 26 m. E of Lausanne. Pop. 2,010. The church, which is built on a hill, occupies the site of the ancient castle of the counts of Gruyère. This town has suffered repeatedly from fire. Since the last conflagration, which occurred in 1800, it has been rebuilt entirely of stone. It has several important cattle-fairs.

CHATEAUDOUBLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Drôme, cant. of Chabeuil, 12 m. E of Valence. Pop. 732. It has some woollen manufactories, and two annual fairs. Of its ancient castle the ruins only now exist.—Also a commune in the dep. of Var, cant. of Callas. Pop. 1,016.

CHATEAU-DU-LOIR, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, arrond. of Saint-Calais. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,713; in 1841, 13,127. The town, called also during the republic Mont-sur-Loir, is near the r. bank of the Loir, 27 m. SW of Saint-Calais, and 26 m. SSE of Mans. Pop. 3,029. It contains a communal college; and has manufactories of linen and leather, and a cotton spinning-mill. The trade consists in cattle, timber, clover-seed, nuts, and wine. Fairs are held six times a-year.

CHATEAUDUN, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Eure - et - Loir. The arrond. comprises an area of 135,885 hect.; and contains 5 cant., viz.: Bonneval, Brou, Chateaudun, Cloyes, and Orgeres. Pop. in 1831, 59,395; in 1841, 62,618; in 1846, 64,249.— The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,814; in 1841, 15,855.—The town, called during the republic Dun-sur-Loir, stands on an elevated plateau, near the l. bank of the Loir. Pop. in 1789, 6,006; in 1821, 5,997; in 1831, 6,461; in 1836, 6,776; in 1841, 6,580; in 1846, 6,680. Its streets, which are broad, are built in straight lines, and terminate in an extensive square. The houses present a generally uniform structure. The college, public library, and town-house are the only modern buildings worthy of note. In the vicinity, on a rocky eminence, is the ancient castle of the counts of Dunois. It contains some manufactories of woollen coverlets, a printing establishment, and tanneries; and has a considerable trade in cattle, grain, wool, timber, clover-seed, and leather. Fairs are held six times a-year. This town was destroyed by fire in 1723.

CHATEAUFORT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Palaiseau, 6 m. S of Versailles. Pop. 524. It derives its name from an ancient fortress, the site of which is now occupied by the village. A fair is held once a-year.
CHATEAU-GARNIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. of Gençais. Pop. 1,002.
CHATEAUGAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Riom. Pop. 993.

CHATEAUGAY, a township of Franklin co., in the state of New York, U.S., 202 m. NW of Albany. It presents a hilly surface, and is drained by a river of the same name and its branches. The soil consists of clay and sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 2,824. -The C. takes its rise 27 m. W of Plattsburg; runs NE into Lower Canada; traverses Huntingdon co.; and after a total course of 70 m. enters the lake of St. Louis, 15 m. SW of Montreal. It is navigable throughout nearly its entire course.

CHATEAUGIRON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of Rennes. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,017; in 1841, 11,971.—The town, which was formerly fortified, is situated on an eminence 10 m. SE of Rennes, on the road from that town to Angers. Pop. in 1846, 1,405. It has manufactories of sail-cloth, and some trade in linen and CHA thread. It was taken by the Leaguers in 1590 and 1592.
CHATEAU-GOMBERT, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-des-Rhone, com. and 4 m. NE of Marseilles. Pop. 1,265. It affords excellent wine. Kermes are found on the adjacent hills.

CHATEAU-GONTIER, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne. The arrond comprises an area of 121,648 hect.; and contains 6 cant., viz.: Saint-Aignan-sur- Roe, Bierné, Château - Gontier, Cosse - le - Vivien, Craon, and Grez-en-Bouëre. Pop. in 1831, 72,888; in 1841, 74,741; in 1846, 77,639.—The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 19,135; in 1841, 20,052.—The town lies in a fertile locality, on the Mayenne, by which it is separated from its principal suburb, 19 m. S of Laval. Pop. in 1789, 3,198; in 1836, 6,226; in 1846, 6,485. It is well built; and contains a church—a handsome Gothic structure—a communal college, three alms-houses, public baths, &c. It has manufactories of linen and woollen fabrics, several tanneries and bleacheries, and a considerable trade in cattle, clover-seed, linseed, grain, wine, salt, timber, white-iron, slate, turf and coal, linen, and thread. It has several annual fairs.
CHATEAU-GRILLET, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Loire, com. of Saint-Michel. The environs afford excellent wine.
CHATEAU-GUIBERT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Mareuil. Pop. 1,020.
CHATEAU-HAUT-BRION, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Pujols, com. of Pessac.
CHATEAU-LAFFITTE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and com. of Pauillac. It is celebrated for its red Bordeaux wines.

CHATEAU-LANDON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, arrond. of Fontainebleau. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,966; in 1841, 10,565.—The town stands on a hill, on the l. bank of the Suzain, 18 m. S of Fontainebleau. Pop. in 1846, 2,336. It has manufactories of Spanish whiting and serges, and a considerable trade in wine and grain. Fairs are held five times a-year. In the environs are extensive quarries of fine freestone.
CHATEAU-LATOUR, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and com. of Pauillac.

CHATEAU - LA - VALLIERE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Indreet-Loire, arrond. of Tours. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,175; in 1841, 11,231.—The town is 21 m. NW of Tours. Pop. in 1846, 1,370. It contains some handsome houses; and has some trade in linen, timber, and cattle. In the environs are several blast-furnaces and forges.
